# Governance Object Lifecycle

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every governance object SHOULD progress through defined lifecycle stages:

| Stage | Meaning | Entry Criteria |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| **Assess** | Under evaluation. Not yet committed to. | Object created with initial content |
| **Trial** | Active experiment. Being tested in practice. | Assessment complete, sponsor identified |
| **Adopt** | Committed. In active use. Enforced. | Trial successful, governance review passed |
| **Hold** | Adopted but frozen — no new adoption. Under review. | Concerns raised, review triggered |
| **Rejected** | Evaluated and declined. Preserved for record. | Assessment or trial concluded negatively |
| **Retire** | Previously adopted, now superseded or obsolete. | Replacement identified, migration complete |

**Transitions are governed events.** Moving between stages requires documented
rationale and is logged. No object silently moves between stages.

## What Good Looks Like

- Every governance object has a visible stage label.
- Stage transitions are logged with rationale.
- Objects in "hold" have a review date and owner.
- Retired objects reference their replacement.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-C11 (Evolutionary Optimization) — lifecycle stages ARE evolutionary states
- JOS-C07 (Knowledge Decay) — lifecycle management prevents decay
- JOS-R04 (Review Currency) — review cadence enforcement supports lifecycle

### Framework Connection

Framework §9 (lifecycle states as an orthogonal axis rather than a linear progression) enriches the lifecycle standard by making lifecycle status independent of adoption stage — an object can be in any lifecycle state regardless of its stage label.
